title: Hieracium lasiochaetum
intro: Hieracium lasiochaetum, also known as hairy hawkweed, is a plant species within the dandelion-like hawkweed group, characterized by its yellow flowers and hairy leaves. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, including its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, which can aid in wound healing and reduce fever. The bioactive constituents of Hieracium lasiochaetum include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to make herbal preparations such as infusions, tinctures, and salves, which are used to treat various health conditions, including skin irritations and infections.
taxonomy: Hieracium lasiochaetum is a species of flowering plant in the dandelion family, Asteraceae. It is a type of hawkweed, a genus of plants known for their yellow flowers. The classification of Hieracium lasiochaetum is further defined by its membership in the tribe Senecioneae, which includes many related species of dandelions and hawkweeds. As a member of the plant kingdom, Hieracium lasiochaetum is also classified as a member of the kingdom Plantae. Further classification includes being a member of the subfamily Cichorioideae.
constituents: Hieracium lasiochaetum is a species of plant, but I couldn't find any information on its specific active constituents. However, plants in the Hieracium genus are known to contain sesquiterpene lactones, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. These compounds may also contribute to the plant's antimicrobial and anticancer effects. Further research is needed to fully understand the active constituents of Hieracium lasiochaetum. More studies are required to determine the exact compounds responsible for its biological activities.
